parties: Mema S.R.L. Società Agricola v Community Plant Variety Office
title: CPVO BoA A014/2020 P - Braeburn 78
text:
CPVO BOARD OF APPEAL PROCEDURAL DECISION DATED 9 NOVEMBER 2022 
concerning an objection raised p ursuant to Article 48 of Regula tion (EC) No 2100/94   
in Case A014/2020 regarding Community Plant Variety Right Appli cation No 2009/0954  
(Variety denomination ‘Braeburn 78’, Species ‘Malus domestica B orkh.) 
 
 
concerning:   Mema S.R.L. Società Agricola    ‘Appellant’ or ‘Party Raising t he Objection’   
Via Chiesa 54 39018 Terlano (BZ) Italy         Represented by: Wuesthoff & Wuesthoff Patentanwälte PartG mbB 
Schweigerstraße 2 
81541 München Germany   Community Plant Variety Office   ‘CPVO’ or ‘Party Opposing the Objection’  
3 Boulevard Maréchal Foch 49000 Angers  France 
 
On 16 May 2022 the Appellant in Case A014/2020 lodged an object ion (hereinafter “Objectio n”) pursuant to Article 
48 of Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 of 27 July 1994 on Community p lant variety rights (OJ 1994 L 227, p. 1) 
(hereinafter “Basic Regulation” or “BR”) before the Registry of  the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety 
Office (hereinafter “Registry”).   
 
The CPVO Board of Appeal, composed of Mr Marcus Navin-Jones as Alternate Chairperson, Mr Hidde Koenraad as 
the Legally Qualified Member; and Professor Dr Dirk Reheul as t he Technically Qualified Mem ber, gives the following 
Decision: The Objection is dismissed.  
 
I. Form of action sought 
 1. The Appellant requests the CPVO Board of Appeal in its alter nate composition and conve ned for the purposes of 
assessing the admissibility and merits of the Objection, includ ing the action to be taken concerning the Objection 
( h e r e i n a f t e r  “ A l t e r n a t e  B o a r d ” ) ,  t o  r e m o v e  t h e  C h a i r p e r s o n  f r o m  adjudicating in Case A014/2020. The CPVO 
r e q u e s t s  t h e  A l t e r n a t e  B o a r d  t o  d i s m i s s  t h e  O b j e c t i o n ,  o r  c o n s i der it as inadmissible, and not to remove the 
Chairperson.  
II. Summary of the facts 
 2. On 19 October 2020, the CPVO adopted a decision maintaining the refusal of the application for a Community 
Plant Variety Right in relation to Application 2009/0954 ‘Braeb urn 78’ (hereinaf ter “Contested Decision”).  
 3 .  O n  1 7  D e c e m b e r  2 0 2 0 ,  t h e  A p p e l l a n t  l o d g e d  a n  a p p e a l  b e f o r e  t he CPVO Board of Appeal concerning the 
Contested Decision.    4. On 19 February 2021, the Appellant submitted to the Registry  its Statement of Grounds.  
 
5. On 12 March 2021, following its review of the case, the CPVO  Rectification Committee decided not to rectify the 
Contested Decision.  

  
 2 / 13
 
6. On 22 April 2021, the appeal case was remitted to the CPVO B oard of Appeal. 
 7. On 1 February 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an email  inviting the Appellant to indicate whether it would 
like an oral hearing to be held and proposing the date of 25 Ma rch 2022. On the same date, i.e. 1 February 2022, 
the Appellant sent the Registry an email indicating that it did  want an oral hearing to be held, but proposing the 
oral hearing take place in May 2022.   8. On 4 February 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an email  proposing the oral hearing be held on 17 May 2022. 
On the same date, i.e. 9 February 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an email confirming its availability to attend 
an oral hearing on the proposed date. On the same date, the Reg istry sent the Appellant a  second email confirming 
the date of the oral hearing as 17 May 2022.   9. On 15 April 2022, the CPVO Board of Appeal served on the par ties a summons to attend the oral hearing to be 
held by video conference (herein after “Oral Hearing”). The date  and time of the Oral Hearing was set at 17 May 
2022 at 9.30 a.m.. The parties were informed, amongst other thi ngs, that a ‘dry run’ would be held on Monday 16 
May 2022 at 9.00 a.m., to ensure the videoconference line and f acilities functioned as required, before the Oral 
Hearing. The parties were also invited, amongst other things, t o provide pleading notes to the Board of Appeal 
before 13 May 2022.    10. On 29 April 2022, an internal email was sent by the Technic ally Qualified Member of the CPVO Board of Appeal 
to the Registry, copying others, stating “
The BOA in case A014/2020 (Braeb urn 78) wants to submit a numbe r of 
questions addressed to the CPVO Office and the Examination Offi ce to be dealt with during the hearing of 17 May 
2022. You can find the questions in the attachment. Can you pro vide our questions to these parties? Can you 
confirm the reception of these questions ? Best regards [sender  name and contact details] ”. 
 11. On 2 May 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO and the relevant examination office (GEVES/INRAE) an email with 
an attachment containing a number of technical questions (herei nafter “Board’s Technical Questions” or “BTQs”) 
and stating that the Board’s  Technical Questions were “
to be dealt with during the oral hearing of 17 May 2022 ”.  
 12. On 13 May 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an email in forming the Board of Appeal that the Appellant was 
“
considering ” whether it attended the Oral Hearing or not. On the same date , the Registry sent an email to the 
Appellant requesting clarificatio n on the Appellant’s communica tion. The Appellant subsequently sent the Registry 
a second email at 16.10 with a letter attached. The email state d, amongst other things, “ Please immediately pass 
on this letter to the Board, there is no need for a dry run [to  ensure the videoconference li ne and facilities functioned 
properly] on Monday morning ”. The attached letter stated, amongst other things, that the A ppellant “ will not attend 
the oral proceedings and withdraws the request for oral proceed ings” and set out a number of pleas and arguments 
concerning Case A014/2020 (hereinafter “Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022”).  
 13. On the same date, i.e. 13 May 2022, the Registry sent the C PVO an email informing the CPVO that the Appellant 
no longer wished to have an Oral Hearing, and providing the CPV O a copy of the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022.  
 14. On the same date, i.e. 13 May 2022, at 17.59, the CPVO sent  the Registry an email attaching 4 documents, 
specifically: (1) 1 document containing written answers to the Board’s Technical Questions Document with 2 
documents affixed as annexes (“CPVO’s Response to the BTQs”); a nd (2) a document entitled ‘CPVO Pleading 
Notes’. 
 
15. On 16 May 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO an email setting  the deadline for the CPVO to provide comments 
on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022.  
 16. On the same date, i.e. 16 May 2022 at 12.31, the Registry s ent the Appellant an email stating “
[f]or the sake of 
completeness, the Board would like to share with you the CPVO a nswers to the Board’s questions prior to the oral 
hearing ”, and attaching the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs.  
 17. On the same date, i.e. 16 May 2022, the Appellant sent an e mail to the Registry, and t o the CPVO, enclosing 1 
attachment. The attachment was a letter addressed to the Chairp erson of the CPVO Board of Appeal and raising an 
objection pursuant to Article 48 of the Basic Regulation (herei nafter “Objection Letter”).  
 

  
 3 / 13
18. On 17 May 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an email: ( 1) stating that “ …[o]nly through your letter [i.e. the 
Appellant’s Objection Letter], the Board became aware of the fa ct that this document [i.e. the Board’s Technical 
Questions] was not sent to you before. This has clearly been ca used by a human error for which the Board 
apologises ”; (2) inviting the Appellant to provide observations on the Bo ard’s Technical Questions and the CPVO’s 
Response to the BTQs within 3 months; and (3) stating that, by raising an objection pursuant to Article 48(3) of the 
Basic Regulation there may be “ a serious delay in the current proceedings ” and requesting confirmation from the 
Appellant that the Appellant wished to continue to raise the Ob jection. On the same date, the Appellant sent the 
Registry an email stating that t he objection it had raised purs uant to Article 48(3) of the  Basic Regulation remained 
“unchanged ” and that the Appellant would “ try to accommodate the 3-month time limit ”.  
 
19. On the same date, i.e. 17 May 2022, the Appellant submitted  to  the CPVO a request fo r public access to 
documents concerning the case-file in Case A014/2020.  20. On 25 May 2022, the Registry sent the parties in the appeal  proceedings an email conc erning the most recent 
procedural developments and next steps in the main proceedings regarding Case A014/2020. The email informed 
the parties, amongst other things, that the Board of Appeal had : (1) provided the Appella nt the Board’s Technical 
Questions and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs and invited the A ppellant to provide observations on those 
documents; and ( 2)  reso lved not to  include the document entitle d ‘CPVO Pleading Notes’ and received by the 
Registry from the CPVO on 16 May 2022, in the appeal case file,  and that the document would not be considered 
by the Board of Appeal, as no oral hearing was currently schedu led.   
 21. On 2 June 2022, the CPVO sent the Registry an email request ing a time extension for the CPVO to provide 
observations on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022.   22. On 7 June 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO an email informi ng the CPVO that a time extension to provide 
observations on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022 was granted .  
 23. On 8 June 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant documents c oncerning the case-file in Case A014/2020.   
 
24. On 18 July 2022, the CPVO sent the Registry an email with a  document attached. The attached document 
contained the CPVO’s observations on the Appellant Letter of 13  May 2022.  
 25. On 10 August 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an email  with a letter attached, requesting a time extension 
t o  t h e  d e a d l i n e  f o r  t h e  A p p e l l a n t  to provide observations on th e Board’s Technical Questions and the CPVO’s 
Response to the BTQs.   26. On 11 August 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an email  informing the Appellant that the deadline for the 
Appellant to provide observations on the Board’s Technical Ques tions and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs, had 
been extended.  27. On 26 August 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an email  with a document attached. The document contained 
the Appellant’s observations on the Board’s Technical Questions  and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs.    
 
III. Procedure following the lo dgement of the Objection 
  28. As stated at paragraph 17 above, on 16 May 2022 the Appella nt lodged, with the Registry, an objection pursuant 
to Article 48 of the Basic Regulation, by way of a letter. The Objection was directed at the Chairperson of the CPVO 
Board of Appeal.   29. On 20 May 2022, the Board of Appeal resolved to convene a p anel of members to examine the Objection 
comprising Mr Marcus Navin-Jones as Alternate Chairperson, Mr H idde Koenraad as the Legally Qualified Member; 
and Professor Dr Dirk Reheul as the Technically Qualified Membe r. 
 30. On 25 May 2022, the Registry sent the parties in the appeal  proceedings an email concerning the Objection with 
an attachment. The email and attachment: (1) informed the parti es that an Article 48 BR Objection had been lodged; 
(2) set out the action the Alternate Board intended to take fol lowing the lodgement of the Objection; and (3) set 
out a number of questions addres sed to each party concerning th e Objection (hereinafter “Alternate Board’s 
Questions”).   
 

  
 4 / 13
31. On 28 June 2022, the Appellant provided the Registry its re ply to the Alternate B oard’s Questions.  
 32. On 30 June 2022, the CPVO prov ided the Registry its reply t o the Alternate Board’s Questions. 
 33. On 6 July 2022, the Alternate Board invited each party to p rovide its observations on the other party’s replies to 
the Alternate Board’s Questions, and to provide any other comme nts as necessary. The Alternate Board also invited 
the parties to indicate whether they wished to hold a virtual ( on line) meeting to provide oral submissions in relation 
to the Objection.   34. On 5 August 2022, the CPVO provided the Registry its observ ations on the replies and informed the Registry 
that the CPVO did not request an oral hearing regarding the Obj ection.  
 35. On 26 August 2022, the Appellant provided the Registry its observations on the replies. 
 36. On 2 September 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an ema il giving the Appellant more time to indicate 
whether the Appellant requested a virtual (on line) meeting to provide oral submissions regarding the Objection.  
 37. On 7 September 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an ema il with a letter attached, informing the Registry 
that the Appellant did not request an oral hearing regarding th e Objection.  
 38. On 14 September 2022, as none of the parties requested an o nline (virtual) oral hearing concerning the 
Objection, and as the Alternate Board considered it had suffici ent information to decide on the Objection, the parties 
were informed that no online (virtual) oral hearing concerning the Objection would be convened, and that the 
Alternate Board would proceed with issuing a Decision concernin g the Objection in due course.   
 
IV. Admissibility 
 39. The Appellant asserts that the Objection is admissible. The  Appellant submits that no procedural step has been 
taken rendering the Objection in admissible pursuant to Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation. According to the 
Appellant, the statements contained within its Objection Letter  calling upon the CPVO Board of Appeal to “
grant 
[the Appellant] a sufficient  written submission period ” to provide written observations on the Board’s Technical 
Questions and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs did not “ constitute a request or opinion within the meaning of the 
second sentence of Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation ”. The Appellant asserts that these statements were “ merely 
an indication that the Board of Appeal must give the Appellant an opportunity to comment on the questions raised 
by the Board of Appeal and the CPVO’s replies ”.  
 40. The CPVO takes the view that the Objection should be regard ed as inadmissible. That is because, according to 
the CPVO, a procedural step has been taken which renders the Ob jection as inadmissible. In the CPVO’s view, 
according to a literal and teleological interpretation of Artic le 48(3) of the Basic Regulation, the term “
procedural 
step” should be interpreted in a broad manner to include, amongst o ther things, “ the appellant’s submission of 
observations to the CPVO’s answers to the Board’s Technical Que stions ” and, in addition, “ a…step as that in the 
present case, namely, a step based on which the concerned party  is afforded of a sort of “remedy” for the action or 
reason that raised his/her concern of partiality ”.   
 
Findings of the Board 
 
41. Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation states: 
 
Members of the Boards of Appeal may be objected to by any party  to the appeal proceedings for one of 
the reasons mentioned in paragraph 1, or if suspected of partia lity. An objection shall not be admissible if, 
while being aware of a reason for objecting, the party to the a ppeal proceedings has taken a procedural 
step. No objection may be based on the nationality of members. 
 42. It is the Appellant, who is a party to the appeal proceedin gs, who has raised an object ion under Article 48(3) of 
the Basic Regulation in this case.  43. As the Appellant clarified in its reply to the Alternate Bo ard’s Questions, the Objection has been raised against 
the Chairperson, and the Chairperson alone, who is a member of the CPVO Board of Appeal. 
 

  
 5 / 13
44. The reason for the Objection concerns a suspicion of partia lity. As the Appellant clari fied in its reply to the 
Alternate Board’s Questions, the Appellant has not raised the O bjection for any of the reasons set out in paragraph 
1 of Article 48 of the Basic Regulation, or for any other reaso n. The Alternate Board therefore concludes that it is 
the time at which the Appellant became aware of the suspicion o f partiality, and whether o r not the Appellant took 
a procedural step prior to this point, which is of primary lega l importance. In this regard the Alternate Board notes 
that the Boards of Appeal of the European Patent Office (herein after “EPO”) have concluded that, in a situation 
where a party had raised a partiality objection before the EPO Boards of Appeal and then, subsequently, lodged 
certain new requests after the objection proceedings under Arti cle 24(4) of the European Patent Convention 1973 
had commenced – those requests did not render the partiality ob jection inadmissible (See Case T 1020/06 of 15 
May 2009 ECLI:EP:BA:2009:T102006.20090515). 
 
45. The suspicion of partiality in this case is based on the ev ents concerning the Board’s Technical Questions and 
the Oral Hearing, and the subsequent and related issues. The Ap pellant became aware of the Board’s Technical 
Questions on 16 May 2022. The Appellant raised the Objection on  the same day, i.e. 16 May 2022.  
 46. The Alternate Board notes that the Appellant raised the Obj ection by way of the Objection Letter which consisted 
of 2 parts: the first part containing the Objection itself, the  second and subsequent part setting out certain points 
regarding the future conduct of the appeal proceedings. Althoug h comprising of 2 parts, the Board of Appeal 
considers that the und erlying intention and purpose of the Obje ction Letter was clear – namely to raise an objection 
pursuant to Article 48 of the Basic Regulation. Moreover, the A lternate Board finds that at the time the Appellant 
became aware of the suspicion of partiality, the Appellant had not taken a relevant procedural step. As such, the 
Alternate Board finds that the conditions concerning admissibil ity were, in this regard, met. Developments in the 
a p pe al  c a se  fo llo w i ng  t h e s ubm is sio n  o f  t h e  O bj ect io n t o  the  CP VO Board of Appeal, including the Appellant’s 
responses to requests and invitations from the CPVO Board of Ap peal, do not call into question this finding, nor the 
admissibility of the Objection in this case.   47. The Alternate Board therefore concludes the Objection is ad missible.  
 
V. Substance 
 48. In support of the Objection, the Appellant relies on the fo llowing pleas and arguments: 
(1) the Appellant rejects the Chairperson pursuant to Article 48 of  the Basic Regulation;  
(2) as it has a suspicion of partiality regarding the Chairperson w hich is reasonable given the facts, the 
Chairperson must be removed; and  
(3) even if the alleged infringement of the procedural rules are re ctified, the Chairperson must still be removed, 
as, following the conduct of the appeal proceedings to-date, th e Appellant cannot expect a fair trial under 
the Chairperson, and the Appellant’s suspicion of partiality wo uld, in any event, continue to persist.  
 First Plea: Appellant’s purported rejection of the Chairperson pursuant to Article 48 of the Basic 
Regulation  49. The Appellant clarified in its Reply to the Alternate Board ’s Questions, that, according to its reading of Article 
48(3) of the Basic Regulation, “
members of the Boards of Appeal may be rejected by any party to  the appeal 
proceedings on the basis of a suspicion of partiality ”. According to the Appellant, if there is a relevant suspicion  of 
partiality “ the legal consequence of Article 48(1) of the Basic Regulation shall apply [i.e.]  ‘The members of the 
Boards of Appeal shall not participate in appeal proceedings,.. .’.” The Appellant references the German version of 
the legal text of Article 48 of the Basic Regulation, which sta tes, in relevant part:  
 
Artikel 48 
Ausschließung und Ablehnung 
[…] 
(3) Die Mitglieder der Beschwerdekammern können von jedem Betei ligten am Beschwerdeverfahren aus 
einem der in Absatz 1 genannten Gründe oder wegen Besorgnis der  Befangenheit abgelehnt werden.   
 
50. The CPVO rejects this plea and the related lines of argumen t.  
 
  

  
 6 / 13
Findings of the Board  
 
51. By the Appellant’s first plea and related arguments, the Al ternate Board understands that, according to the 
Appellant, Article 48 of the Basic Regulation affords any party  to appeal proceedings the right to reject a member 
of the CPVO Board of Appeal if that party, amongst other things , has a relevant suspicion of partiality relating to the 
member in question. According to the Appellant, as it has raise d an objection regarding suspected partiality directed 
at the Chairperson in this case satisfying the relevant legal c riterion (i.e. the applicant has a relevant suspicion of 
partiality), the Chairperson must be removed pursuant to Articl e 48 of the Basic Regulation.  
   52. The Alternate Board observes that the examination of the fi rst plea requires the interpretation of Article 48(3) 
of the Basic Regulation. 
 53. As a preliminary remark, the Alternate Board recalls that, in determining the scope of a provision of European 
Union law, regard must, as a general rule, first be had to its wording (see, for example, Case T-521/14, 
Sweden v 
Commission ,  E U : T : 2 0 1 5 : 9 7 6 ,  p a r a g r a p h  5 9 ;  s e e  a l s o ,  t o  t h a t  e f f e c t ,  C a s e  C -383/14, Sodiaal International , 
EU:C:2015:541, paragraphs 20 and 24). Where, on the basis of th e  w o r d i n g  a l o n e ,  t h e  t e x t  i s  n o t ,  i n  i t s e l f ,  
sufficiently clear, regard must be had to the context and objec tives of the provision in question (see, for example, 
Case C-453/14, Vorarlberger Gebietskrankenkasse , EU:C:2016:37, paragraph 27 and the case-law cited; Case T-
521/14, Sweden v Commission , EU:T:2015:976, paragraph 57 and the case-law cited).  
 54. The Alternate Board also recalls that when assessing the wo rding of a provision of European Union law, it is 
important to bear in mind that European Union legislation is dr afted in several languages and that the different 
language versions are all equall y authentic. An interpretation of a provision of Community law thus involves a 
comparison of the different language versions (see, for example , Case 283/81, 
Srl CILFIT and Lanificio di Gavardo 
SpA v Ministry of Health , EU:C:1982:335, paragraph 18). The necessity for uniform appli cation, and accordingly 
interpretation, of European Union law makes it impossible to co nsider one version of the legal text in isolation but 
requires that it be interpreted on the basis of, in particular,  the different language versions (see, Case 29/69, Erich 
Stauder v City of Ulm – Sozialamt , EU:C:1969:57, paragraph 3). Where there is a divergence betwe en the various 
language versions of the EU legislative text, the need for unif orm interpretation and app lication requires that the 
provision in question be interpreted by reference to the purpos e and general scheme of the rules which it forms 
part (see CPVO Board of Appeal Procedural Decision in Joined ap peal cases A007/2009, A002/2010 and A003/2010, 
Sumost 02, paragraph 44 and the case law cited therein).   55. In order to assess the wording of Article 48(3) of the Basi c Regulation, the Alternate Board therefore finds it 
necessary to assess the German version of the legal text and, i n addition, the other (non German) versions of the 
legal text.  56. As regards the German version of the legal text, the Altern ate Board observes, amongst other things, the use 
of the terms “
Ablehnung ” and “ abgelehnt ” in Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation. The Alternate Board  notes that, 
given, in particular, the wordin g of the German legal text, it could be possible to interpret the German language 
version of the legal text as affording parties to appeal procee dings with a right not to object to the adjudication of 
a member of the CPVO Board of Appeal, but a right to reject  to their adjudication. However, having regard to the 
other non-German language texts of Article 48(3) of the Basic R egulation, in particular the English, Polish, French, 
Spanish and Italian versions, the Alternate Board notes that it  is clear that Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation does 
not afford parties to appeal proceedings with a right to reject  to the adjudication of members of the CPVO Board of 
Appeal, but a right to object  to their adjudication. In accord ance with the necessity for un iform application and 
interpretation of European Union law, the Alternate Board finds  that the German language version of Article 48 of 
the Basic Regulation must be interpreted consistently with the other language versions of the legal text and, in 
particular, as affording parties of appeal proceedings with a r ight to object, but not a right to reject, to the 
adjudication of members of the CPVO Board of Appeal, where the relevant legal conditions are met.  
 57. To the extent there is a divergence of meaning between the German version and the other language versions 
of Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation, the Alternate Board n otes that Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation must be 
interpreted by reference to the purpose and general scheme of t he rules which it forms part. In this regard the 
Alternate Board concludes that to interpret Article 48(3) of th e Basic Regulation as affording parties to an appeal 
proceeding with a suspicion of p artiality a right to remove or reject a member of the CPVO Board of Appeal from 
acting on an appeal case – would be inconsistent with the schem e of procedural rules it forms part of. That is 
because, as the CPVO Board of Appeal has already concluded prev iously: “…
that interpretation would lead to a 
situation where a mere claim of partiality against one or sever al members of the Board of Appeal would lead to their 

  
 7 / 13
substitution by alternate members. This would not only give the  parties the possibility to modify the composition of 
the Board of Appeal at their own will but would also make the f irst sentence of paragraph 4 of Article 48 devoid of 
meaning” (see CPVO Board of Appeal Procedural Decision in Joined appeal cases A007/2009, A002/2010 and 
A003/2010, Sumost 02, paragraph 45).  58. It follows from the above th at, a party to an appeal procee ding has the right to raise an objection pursuant to 
Article 48 of the Basic Regulation against a member of the CPVO  Board of Appeal, but no right to reject a member 
of the CPVO Board of Appeal from adjudication. As the CPVO has correctly stated in these proceedings, it is for the 
CPVO Board of Appeal to determine whether an objection raised p ursuant to Article 48 of the Basic Regulation is, 
amongst other things, admissible and well founded and, in addit ion, and as stated in first sentence of Article 48(4) 
of the Basic Regulation, 
“the action to be taken in the cases specified in paragraphs 2 and 3 [of Article 48 of the 
Basic Regulation]… ”. The Appellant’s purported rejection of the Chairperson in th is case is therefore not well founded 
and must be dismissed.     59. Given the above, the Alternate Board concludes that the App ellant’s first plea must be rejected. 
 Second Plea: Appellant’s assertion that it has a suspicion of p artiality regarding the Chairperson 
which is reasonable given the facts  6 0 .  T h e  A p p e l l a n t  a s s e r t s  t h a t  i t  h a s  a  s u s p i c i o n  o f  p a r t i a l i t y  r e g a r d i n g  t h e  C h a i r p e r s o n  in this case which is 
reasonable given the facts and, as such, the Chairperson must b e removed from adjudicating in this case. In support 
of its plea, the Appellant makes the following arguments.   
1. The Appellant has a suspicion of partiality  
 
61. According to the Appellant, the core question concerning th e Appellant’s Objection raised pursuant to Article 48 
of the Basic Regulation, is “ whether there is a ‘suspicion of partiality’ ”. The Appellant states that, in this case, it is 
the “person concerned ” and only it could be expected to have a suspicion of partiali ty. The Appellant states that “ it 
is obvious that the CPVO assess the present situation, in which  the allegation is that there is a bias in favour of the 
CPVO and to the detriment of the Appellant, unlike the Appellan t”. The Appellant states that the Appellant’s point 
of view is therefore “ relevant ”, that the CPVO’s view “ does not matter ” and that “ it is irrelevant whether the CPVO 
considers that there is bias or not ”. According to the Appellant, the Alternate Board must “ assess whether the 
applicant has a ‘suspicion of partiality’ ” which must be done “ on the basis of th e underlying facts ”. This, according 
to the Appellant, is “ a legal decision ” which the legally competent members of the Alternate Board mu st answer 
independently. Whether there is a suspicion of partiality does not, according to the Appellant, concern whether there 
are “serious doubts ”. In the Appellant’s view “ no actual partiality is required ”.  Acco rd in g  to  th e  Ap pe ll an t,  the 
A l t e r n a t e  B o a r d  m u s t  t h e r e f o r e  e x a m i n e  w h e t h e r ,  f r o m  t h e  A p p e l l a n t ’ s  p o i n t  o f  v i e w ,  f o l l o w i n g  a  r e a s o n a b l e  
assessment of all the circum stances, there are doubts  as to the impartiality and independence of the Chairperson.   
 62. The Appellant asserts that, in this case, it does have a su spicion of impartiality and th at this suspicion is based 
on a reasonable assessment of the underlying facts. According t o the Appellant, a suspicion of partiality may arise 
where “
it can be concluded, from an objective point of view” that procedural errors are due to, or explained by, an 
unjustified approach towards one of the parties, or an unjustif ied, targeted investigatio n into the collection of 
evidence outside the formal proceedings and where the facts giv e the justified impression that there is partiality. 
The Appellant states that it has a suspicion of partiality “ on a reasonable basis ” even if the Board of Appeal did not 
wish to create that impression. The Appellant further states th at its fear of the existence of partiality is “ even 
confirmed ” by the “ instructions ” provided by the Technically Qualified Member of the Board of Appeal to the Registry 
that the Board’s Technical  Questions should not be “ forwarded to the Appellant ”.   
 
2. The Appellant’s suspic ion of partiality is r easonable given the  facts and circumstances  
 63. The Appellant argues that it has a “
valid” suspicion of partiality as the suspicion is based on the fact s, which 
include breaches of “ fundamental procedural principles such as the right to be heard  and a transparent and fair 
trial”. According to the Appellant, the facts “ allow it to be assumed” that the Appellant has a suspicion of partiality 
which is reasonable. According to the Appellant, the supporting  evidence includes the following: 
(1) On 29 April 2022, the Technically Qualified Member of the B oard sent an “ instruction ” to the Registry to send 
certain technical questions to “ only one party [in the appeal proceedings]”.  
(2) On 2 May 2022, the Board of Appeal sent a list of questions  to the CPVO and other parties, informing those 
parties that the questions would be dealt with at the Oral Hear ing (Board’s Technical Questions). The Board of 

  
 8 / 13
Appeal did not notify the Appellant “ simultaneously ”, but, instead only “ after [the Appellant had] announced on 13 
May 2022 that he would not att end the hearing”,  “24 hours ” before the hearing, and only “ for completeness ”. 
(3) The Board of Appeal sent the Board’s Technical Questions to  the “ GEVES and INRAE examination offices ” who 
are two bodies which are not involved in the procedure “ for no apparent reason ”.   
(4) The Board of Appeal failed to provide, and has not provided , reasons as to why the Board of Appeal did not 
provide the Appellant the Board’s Technical Questions initially , and only after the Appellant had withdrawn its request 
for an Oral Hearing.      
(5) The list of questions sent by the Board of Appeal to the CP VO and other parties on 2 May 2022 are “ detailed ”, 
“highly relevant ” and “ not easy to answer, extensive, complex and unanswerable without  f u r t h e r  f a c t u a l  
investigation ”. By providing these questions to the CPVO and other parties i t allowed the CPVO to be “ better 
prepared” and informed at the Oral Hearing than the Appellant. Moreover, the impression is that the Board of Appeal 
wanted the CPVO to say certain things, and provide certain answ ers, at the Oral Hearing, i.e. “ put [answers] into 
the [CPVO’s] mouth ”.  
( 6 )  T h e  B o a r d  o f  A p p e a l  e x c l u d e d  t h e  A p p e l l a n t  f r o m  d i s c u s s i o n s  b e t w e e n  t h e  B o a r d  o f  A p p e a l  a n d  t h e  
CPVO/examination office concerning the appeal case “ of highly relevant technical issues ”, prior to the Oral Hearing. 
According to the Appellant, “ i t  w a s  n o t  p l a n n e d ”  t h a t  t h e  A p p e l l a n t  b e  i n v o l v e d  i n  t h o s e  d i s c u s s i o n s  w h i c h  i s  
“confirmed by the official file ”, in particular the communication of 29 April 2022. As such, a ccording to the Appellant, 
the Oral Hearing was set to be held, and would have been held “ taking into account information obtained in this 
way”. The Appellant states that the Oral Hearing was therefore pla nned “ to surprise the Appellant ” on the 
substantive issues and deprive the Appellant of the ability of effectively responding to them in an informed and 
prepared manner. The Appellant submits that even if all the inf ormation and evidence had been fully disclosed 
during the Oral Hearing, the Appellant could not have dealt wit h it in such a way as to ensure the equality of arms 
between the parties due to the “ scope and complexity of the information ”.      
(7) On 13 May 2022, the Board of Appeal received a written resp onse from the CPVO to the Board’s Technical 
Q u e s t i o n s ,  b u t  d i d  n o t  n o t i f y  t h e  A p p e l l a n t  “ immediately on receipt ”. The CPVO’s Response to the BTQs is a 
document consisting of “ 10 pages plus attachments ” and substantive technical submissions.       
(8) The Board of Appeal provided the Appellant the CPVO’s Respo nse to the BTQs “ one day before the date of the 
Oral Hearing, i.e. a date when the preparation of the oral proc eedings by the Board of Appeal and the parties was 
concluded and it was no longer possible to deal with the CPVO’s  reply sufficiently in time ” and only after the Appellant 
had “withdrawn the request for the Oral Hearing ”.     
(9) Had the Appellant attended the Oral Hearing, the Appellant would have been at a “ clear procedural 
disadvantage ” and there would not have been a fair trial.   
(10) The Appellant has “ serious doubts ” whether the Board of Appeal would have sent the Appellant the  CPVO’s 
Response to the BTQs, had the Oral Hearing been held. As the Bo ard of Appeal informed the Appellant of the CPVO’s 
Response to the BTQs “ for completeness ”, the Appellant does not believe so.  
(11) The Board of Appeal failed to provide the Appellant, and h as not provided, the Board’s Technical Questions 
and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs ‘ in the language of the proceedings ’. 
 64. The Appellant also states that some of the alleged infringe ments of EU procedural law, and alleged actions and 
inactions of the Board of Appeal, cannot be regarded as “
human error ”. As regards the Board of Appeal’s alleged 
exclusion of the Appellant from the technical discussions befor e the Oral Hearing putting the CPVO in an alleged 
better informed position than the Appellant, the Appellant stat es that this demonstrates an “ obvious ” violation of 
EU legal principles concerning due process, and that it must be  assumed that these events were not “ coincidence 
or an accident and not a human error ”. The Appellant states that “ There can be no mention of human error here ”. 
Instead, the Appellant argues, it must reasonably conclude thes e alleged actions and inactions by the Board of 
Appeal, are due to a difference in treatment by the Board of Ap peal where: “ the CPVO is preferred and the 
complainant is disadvantaged ”. The Appellant states that the correspondence and documentati on between the 
relevant parties confirms the impression that “ it was no coincidence or human error that the [Board’s Technica l 
Questions] were not sent to the Appellant ”. According to the Appellant, the email of 29 April 2022 ‘ instructs [the 
Registry] to forward [the Board’s Technical Questions] to the C PVO and the Examination Body ’ and asks the Registry 
‘to confirm that the CPVO and the examining body have also recei ved these questions ’. According to the Appellant, 
this is evidence that “ it was…not planned to make the [Board’s Technical Questions] av ailable to the Appellant ” and 
that it was planned that “ [the Board’s Technical Questions] should be discussed directly in the oral hearing, without 
the Appellant being given the opportunity to prepare well for i t”.  
 65. The Appellant asserts that, given the evidence, the impress ion that emerges is that there is a “
secret agreement ” 
between the Board of Appeal and the CPVO and that the Appellant  has been a party in proceedings involving 
“collusion ” between the CPVO and the Board of Appeal. According to the Ap pellant, it was “ precisely this impression ” 

  
 9 / 13
and the “ resulting loss of confidence in the impartiality of the institu tions” which Article 48(3) of the Basic Regulation 
intended to counteract.  
 
3. The Appellant has raised the objection against the relevant member of the CPVO Board of Appeal  
 
66. The Appellant has raised the Objection against the Chairper son alone in this case. The Appellant asserts that it 
i s  c o r r e c t  t o  r a i s e  t h e  p a r t i a l i t y  o b j e c t i o n  a g a i n s t  t h e  C h a i r p e r s o n  i n  t h i s  c a s e  a s ,  a m o n g s t  o t h e r  t h i n g s ,  t h e  
Chairperson is “ a legally qualified member of the Board of Appeal ” who is responsible for ensuring that appeal 
proceedings are carried out in accordance with the legal requir ements concerning due process which include “ the 
right to a fair trial ” and “the equality of arms of the parties ”. In this case, the Appellant states that the Chairperson 
“allowed or did not prevent ” the Board of Appeal from discussing facts and asking question s before the Oral Hearing 
with just one of the parties, and by doing so put just one of t he parties in a position informed of the relevant 
individual circumstances. The Appellant states that in this cas e, the Chairperson is responsible for ensuring situations 
do not arise whereby one party is not informed of important iss ues before an Oral Hearing is scheduled to take 
place, particularly where that situation is  “not based on human error ”. The Appellant submits that the Chairperson 
“had the possibility at several times to ensure that the Appella nt was also informed” of the issues to be discussed 
at the Oral Hearing but “ he did not do that ”. According to the Appellant, th e Chairperson would have allow ed the 
Appellant to participate in an oral hearing, while knowing that  the Appellant was deprived of relevant information  
“Colloquially speaking: the chairman would have let the Appella nt run into the open knife. ” The Appellant states 
that it is “ highly unlikely ” that the Board’s Technical Questions would have been sent to the CPVO, and the CPVO 
would have submitted its response, without the Chairperson’s kn owledge as the Technical ly Qualified Member of 
the Board of Appeal would have needed the Chairperson’s approva l before taking action. Nonetheless, even if the 
Chairperson did not know that the Board’s Technical Questions h ad been sent out to the CPVO and other parties, 
and/or that the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs had been received b y the Board of Appeal, the Chairperson could 
and should have: found out that a matter of this gravity had oc curred, informed the parties, and taken appropriate 
measures, but the Chairperson “ did not do that .” 
 67. The CPVO rejects this plea and the related lines of argumen t. 
 
Findings of the Board 
 
68. As a preliminary remark, and whilst remaining cognizant of the clear differences between the CPVO Board of 
Appeal and the EU Courts of Law, and other Boards of Appeal, th e Alternate Board recalls that Articles 6(1) of the 
European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) and Article 47 of th e Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European 
Union (CFR), are generally recog nised as binding standards for proceedings before, for example, the EPO Boards of 
Appeal (see, for example, Case T 1627/09 of 10 October 2013 ECL I:EP:BA:2013:T162709.20131010). As such, 
there should be very strict observance of the requirement for i mpartiality (see Case Law of the EPO Boards of Appeal 
Section III, Part J, Paragraph 1.2). The Alternate Board furthe r notes that, when carrying out their duties, Boards 
of Appeal should have regard to, amongst other provisions, Arti cle 47 of the CFR, (see Judgment of the General 
Court of 20 September 2019, Germany v European Chemicals Agency , T-755/17, EU:T:2019:647, paragraph 57) 
including the genera l legal order.   
 69. Given the general legal orde r, the similarities which exist  between the CPVO Board of Appeal and other Boards 
of Appeal, and given the importance of maintaining the credibil ity and trust which exists  between the CPVO Board 
of Appeal and its stakeholders, the Alternate Board notes that observance of the requiremen t for impartiality by the 
members of the CPVO Board of App eal is of very high importance.  As such, and consistent with the principle of 
good administration which requir es, amongst other things, that relevant bodies examine “
carefully and impartially ” 
all the relevant facts of a case (Judgment of the General Court , 3 October 2019, BASF v European Chemicals Agency , 
T-805/17, EU:T:2019:723, paragraph 57), and in line with Articl e 15(1) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the 
European Union (“TFEU”) concerni ng openness and transparency in  t h e  w o r k  o f  E U  a g e n c i e s  a n d  b o d i e s ,  t h e  
Alternate Board finds that it is incumbent on the CPVO Board of  Appeal to ensure, insofar as it is relevant to the 
CPVO Board of Appeal affairs, that justice and adherence to the  principle of good administration, are not merely 
done, they are seen to be done.       70. As regards to objections bro ught by parties pursuant to Art icle 48 of the Basic Regulat ion, the Alternate Board 
notes the following. First, members of the CPVO Board of Appeal  are a priori presumed to be impartial. Second, the 
burden-of-proof resides with those parties who raise an objecti on. Third, it is incumbent on those parties who raise 
an objection to ensure that the objection itself is sufficientl y reasoned and substantiated. In this regard the Alternate 
Board notes that it is settled case-law that the facts must be capable of supporting and substantiating a suspicion 

  
 10 / 13 
of partiality, in order for an objection concerning partiality to be upheld. If, for example, there are legitimate reasons 
which validate the manner appeal proceedings have been conducte d in the past, a party’s suspicion of partiality 
based on the conduct of the appeal proceedings cannot be regard ed as substantiated sufficiently or at all, and the 
objection cannot be upheld (see to that effect CPVO Board of Ap peal Procedural Decision of 15 May 2012, Ralf 
Schräder v CPVO  relating to Variety denomination Sumost 02, (“Sumost 02 Object ion Decision”) (paragraphs 60-
6 7 )  a n d  O H I M / E U  I P O  B o a r d s  o f  A p p e a l  C a s e s  T  0 3 5 5 / 1 3  o f  1 8  N o v e mber 2015 
(ECLI:EP:BA:2015:T035513.20151118) and T 1028/96 of  15 Septemb er 1999 
(ECLI:EP:BA:1999:T102896.19990915).   71. The Alternate Board therefore finds it necessary to assess the evidence relied on by the Appellant to determine 
w h et he r,  amo ng st  o t h er th i ngs ,  t h e fa ct s ar e ca p abl e a n d su f f ic i en t to  s u bs t a nti at e a nd su p po rt a re as o n a ble 
suspicion of partiality.  72. As regards the communication of 29 April 2022, the Alternat e Board notes the following points.  
 73. First, the communication is not an instruction and does not  contain an instruction. The communication is a 
request to the Registry for certain action to be taken, without  specifying how that action sh ould be taken in practice. 
More specifically, it is a request for the Registry to “
provide [the Board’s Techn ical Questions] questions ” to the 
r el e va n t  p ar tie s.  It  t h er efo re  f ollows that the Appellant’s ass ertions that, for example, this communication: (1) 
contained “ instructions ” that the Board’s Technical Questions should not be forwarded to the Appellant; and/or (2) 
that the communication constituted an “ instruction ” to the Registry to send certain technical questions to “ only one 
party [in the appeal proceedings]” – cannot be sufficiently supported  or substantiated by the facts. 
 74. Second, the communication does not mention the Appellant, a nd does not state whether the Board’s Technical 
Questions should be provided to the Appellant or not. The email  states that the Board of Appeal wishes to raise 
certain questions to be “
dealt with during the [Oral Hearing] ”. It is sent in reply to the communication of 15 April 
2022 containing the summons to th e Oral Hearing, and which ther efore includes the contact details of the parties 
t o  t h e  p r o c e e d i n g s  –  i n c l u d i n g  t h e  A p p e l l a n t ’ s .  I t  t h e r e f o r e  f o llows that the Appellant’s assertions that this 
communication should be interpreted as a deliberate act intende d to exclude or prevent the Appellant from receiving 
the Board’s Technical Questions and, as meaning, for example, t hat: (1) the Board’s Technical Questions should not 
be “forwarded to the Appellant ”; (2) “ it was…not planned to make the [Board’s Technical Questions] av ailable to 
the Appellant ”; (3) this communication confirms that “ it was not planned ” for the Appellant to be included in the 
discussions before the Oral Hearing; that it was planned that “ [the Board’s Technical Questions] should be discussed 
directly in the oral hearing, without the Appellant being given  the opportunity to prepare well for it ”; (4) that the 
Chairperson purposefully “ a l l o w e d  o r  d i d  n o t  p r e v e n t ” the Board of Appeal from discussing facts and asking 
questions before the Oral Hearing with just one of the parties and by doing so intentionally put on of the parties in 
a better informed position than the other; and/or (5) that the Chairperson intended that the Board’s Technical 
Questions were communicated to certain parties in a non bona fi de manner so as to assist the CPVO and the 
Examination office to prepare for the Oral Hearing, and/or to “ put [answers] into the [CPVO’s] mouth ” and/or to 
exclude the Appellant from preparing answers to these questions  – cannot be sufficiently supported or substantiated 
by the facts.     75. Third, the communication was sent by the Technically Qualif ied Member. It was not sent by the Chairperson, 
nor was the communication sent in the name of the Chairperson o r the members of the Board of Appeal. The 
Alternate Board notes, in this regard, and amongst other things , the complimentary close, sender name, and 
signature block contained within  the email. Moreover, the Alter nate Board also notes, in this regard, the legal and 
factual context within which t his communication was sent.  
 76. In appeal cases before the CPVO Board of Appeal, a chairper son is legally required to assign the examination of 
the appeal to one of the members as rapporteur, which “
may include, where appropriate, the taking of evidence ” 
(Article 11(3) of the Proceedings Regulation).  A chairperson m ay, therefore, appoint a technically qualified member 
of the CPVO Board of Appeal to act as rapporteur and assign exa mination to the technically qualified member.  
However, in those cases where a technically qualified member wi shes to obtain certain evidence, it is incumbent 
upon the technically qualified member, the Registry, and other relevant individuals, to obtain the Chairperson’s 
express authorisatio n, including any clarificatio n o f an instru ction, before taking any relevant action to ensure, 
amongst other things, EU law is strictly adhered to. In this ca se, the Alternate Board notes that there was no 
authorisation or instruction from the Chairperson to exclude th e Appellant in the communications concerning the 
scheduled Oral Hearing, or for the Registry not to provide the Board’s Technical Questions to the Appellant, and 
that no such authorisation or instruction can be inferred. The Alternate Board further notes that the evidence in this 

  
 11 / 13 
regard, namely the absence of au thorisation and instruction fro m the Chairperson, cannot be regarded as evidence 
giving rise to a reasonable suspicion of partiality.  Given the se points, the Alternate Board finds that, contrary to the 
A p p e l l a n t ’ s  a s s e r t i o n s ,  t h e  c o m m u n i c a t i o n  o f  2 9  A p r i l  2 0 2 2  c a n n o t  b e  r ega r de d  as  gi vi ng r is e to  a  re a so n abl e 
suspicion of partiality on the part of the Chairperson, and tha t any purported suspicion of partiality cannot be 
sufficiently supported or substa ntiated by the underlying facts . 
 77. Given the above, the Alterna te Board finds that, the commun ication of 29 April 2022, cannot and does not 
substantiate or support, sufficiently or at all, a reasonable s uspicion of partiality on the part of the Chairperson. 
 78. As regards the alleged exclusion of the Appellant from the communications between t he Board of Appeal and 
the CPVO/Examination Office before the Oral Hearing, the Altern ate Board note the following points.  
 79. First, and as regards the Bo ard’s Technical Questions, comm unicated by the Registry to the CPVO and other 
parties on 2 May 2022, the Board of Appeal notes, as a prelimin ary remark, that the Board of Appeal has a right to 
investigate the facts and circumstances surrounding an appeal “
of its own motion ” (see, amongst others, Aurora Srl 
v CPVO (T-140/15) [2017], ECLI:EU:T:2017:830, paras 72-74, 77 a nd Article 51 of the Proceedings Regulation and 
Article 76 of the Basic Regulation) which includes the ability to raise relevant technical questions before an oral 
hearing with the parties. At the same time, and without it bein g necessary to rule on the alleged infringement of 
the principles of due process or right to a fair trial, an issu e which can only, in principle , be decided before the EU 
Courts, the Alternate Board notes that the communication of suc h questions should not dis regard or undermine the 
rights of parties to an appeal proceedings, or put them at a pr ocedural disadvantage. Where the Board of Appeal 
communicates relevant technical questions to one party in the a ppeal proceedings and there is no valid justification 
for an undue delay in communicating the same questions to the o ther party in the appeal proceedings which puts 
one of the party’s in a potentially procedurally advantageous p osition; where the questions  are not provided in the 
relevant language; and/or where the questions are provided to p arties who are not party to the appeal proceedings, 
without the prior knowledge of a party to the appeal proceeding s - there are, in principle, legitimate grounds for 
raising an objection pursuant to Article 48 of the Basic Regula tion which may be sufficient to substantiate and 
support the objection itself.    
 
80. Second, and as regards the C PVO’s Response to the BTQs, the  Alternate Board notes that this document was 
submitted by the CPVO to the Board of Appeal unrequested and wi thout warning, i.e. as a ‘spontaneous submission’. 
Having received this document from the CPVO on Friday 13 May 20 22 at 17.59, the Board of Appeal disclosed this 
document to the Appellant on Monday 16 May 2022 at 12.31. The B oard of Appeal therefore disclosed this document 
to the Appellant within 1 working day of its receipt. It is inc umbent on the Board of Appeal to ensure that documents 
received from parties to an appeal are admissible, can be added  to the case file, and take any further action 
(including the redaction of rele vant confidential information) before disclosure to the other parties to the appeal. 
This is particularly relevant in  light of spontaneous submissio ns from parties which are unrequested and unforeseen 
(see to that effect CPVO Board of Appeal Procedural Decision of  15 May 2012, Ralf Schräder v CPVO  relating to 
Variety denomination S umost 02, paragraphs 50-59). The Alternat e Board notes that 1 working-day to review the 
C P V O ’ s  R e s p o n s e  t o  t h e  B T Q s ,  p a r t i c u l a r l y  i n  l i g h t  o f  i t s  l e n g t h and complexity, and then communicate that 
submission to the Appellant cannot be regarded as inconsistent with the principle of sound administration. The 
Alternate Board also notes that, by the time the Appellant rece ived the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs, the Appellant 
had already withdrawn its request for an Oral Hearing, and prev ented the Oral Hearing from going ahead in practice 
by, amongst other things, cancelling the ‘dry run’ which was ne cessary to ensure the videoconference line and 
facilities functioned as required, before the Oral Hearing. The refore, the Appellant’s submissions concerning the 
communication of the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs “ one day before the date of the Oral Hearing ” and the inability 
for the Appellant to prepare answers to the Board’s Technical Q uestions before the Oral Hearing – are of no material 
weight or relevance – as the Oral Hearing had already been canc elled, and the Appellant had already ensured the 
O r a l  H e a r i n g  c o u l d  n o t ,  i n  p r a c t i c e ,  g o - a h e a d ,  b e f o r e  t h e  B o a r d  of Appeal provided the Appellant the CPVO’s 
Response to the BTQs.   81. Third, the Alternate Board notes that no Oral Hearing was h eld on 16 May 2022, and no oral hearing has been 
held in the appeal proceedings. The Alternate Board further not es that had an oral hearing taken place, the Appellant 
cannot know with certainty what would, or would not, have happe ned, and whether and how the alleged procedural 
deficiencies, and concerns regarding a fair trial, would have b een addressed. The Appellant’s assertions that, for 
example, the Oral Hearing was set to be held, and would have be en held, in a manner “
to surprise the Appellant ” 
on the substantive issues and not in accordance with the right to a fair trial, or in accordance with the equality of 
arms – are merely suppositions, and cannot be sufficiently supp orted or substantiated by the facts.  
 

  
 12 / 13 
82. Fourth, on the 16 May 2022 the Appellant lodged its Objecti on with the CPVO Board of Appeal. On 17 May 2022, 
the Registry sent an email to the Appellant stating “ [O]nly through your letter, the Board became aware of the fact 
that the [Board’s Technical Questions] was not sent to you befo re. This has clearly been caused by a human error 
for which the Board apologizes ”. That email also proposed immediate corrective action, in par ticular, it included an 
invitation for the Appellant to provide written observations re sponding to the Board’s Technical Questions, and the 
CPVO’s Response to the BTQs, within 3 months. The Alternate Boa rd therefore notes that, having become aware 
of the potential mistake in not informing the Appellant of the Board’s Technical Questions, the Chairperson, as well 
as the other members of the Board of Appeal, took immediate, de cisive and proportionate corrective action and did 
so in a transparent and clear manner. Moreover, the Alternate B oard notes that it can find no reason why this 
proposed corrective action does not address or remedy the alleg ed procedural defects earlier in the appeal 
proceedings and/or why this action does not redress any procedu ral advantage the CPVO may have had earlier in 
the appeal proceedings. Lastly, in this regard, the Alternate B oard notes that the corrective action proposed and 
taken by the Chairperson, and other members of the Board of App eal, was similar, if not the same, as the corrective 
action the Appellant itself had suggested and described in its Objection Letter. It therefore  follows that the Appellant 
cannot now take the view, that the corrective action taken is n ot sufficient or adequate to remedy the potential 
procedural irregularities that o ccurred earlier in the process – or that by taking this action the facts substantiate or 
support the view that a reasonab le suspicion of partiality conc erning the Chairperson arises. 
 83. Fifth, the Alternate Board notes the practical and legal co nsequences following the cancellation of the Oral 
Hearing. In particular the Alternate Board notes that the CPVO and the Examination Office had from 2 May 2022 
u n t i l  1 7  M a y  2 0 2 2  ( i . e .  a p p r o x i m a t e l y  1 5  c a l e n d a r  d a y s ) ,  t o  p r e pare oral submissions concerning the Board’s 
Technical Questions. In contrast, the Appellant has been given,  in total, from 17 May 2022 until 31 August 2022 
(i.e. approximately 107 calendar days), to provide written obse rvations on the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs, and 
the Board’s Technical Questions, in part due to the language ve rsions provided. The Alternate Board therefore 
concludes that the Appellant has not been put at any procedural  disadvantage as regards, for example, its ability to 
effectively make known its views on the Board’s Technical Quest ions and/or the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs, or 
relating to the failure to notify the Appellant of the Board’s Technical Questions before the Oral Hearing scheduled 
for 17 May 2022, which did not take place. 
 
84. Given the above, the Alternate Board finds that, the events  leading up to the Oral Hearing scheduled for 17 May 
2022, when considered together and in particular in light of th e corrective action taken by the Chairperson, and the 
other members of the Board of Appeal, on 17 May 2022 and therea fter, cannot and do not substantiate or support, 
sufficiently or at all, a reasona ble suspicion of  partiality on  the part of the Chairperson. 
 8 5 .  I n  l i g h t  o f  t h e  a b o v e ,  t h e  A l t e r n a t e  B o a r d  c o n c l u d e s  t h a t  t h e  A p p e l l a n t ’ s  s e c o n d  p l e a  m u s t  t h e r e f o r e  b e  
dismissed.  Third plea: According to the Appellant, even if the alleged inf ringement of the procedural rules are 
rectified, the Chairperson must still be removed, as, following  the conduct of the appeal proceedings 
to-date, the Appellant cannot expect a fair trial under the Cha irperson, and the Appellant’s suspicion 
of partiality would continue to persist.  86. The Appellant asserts, that as the Appellant was, amongst o ther things, deprived of extensive and complex 
information relevant to the appeal proceedings before the sched uled date of the Oral Hearing “
the Appellant must 
come to the conclusion that it cannot expect a fair trial under  the Chairperson ”. The Appellant states that, even if 
the potential procedural flaws and deficiencies are addressed a nd rectified, it would not render the Objection itself 
as null and void, as, “ after all, even [if the infringement of the procedural rules ar e rectified] then the suspicion of 
partiality persists ”. The Appellant states that in order to ensure that the procee dings are conducted correctly in the 
rule of law, the person responsible for complying with a rule o f law procedure, “ namely the chairperson of the Board 
of Appeal ” as a legally competent member “ must therefore be removed ”.  
 87. The CPVO rejects this plea and the related lines of argumen t. 
 
Findings of the Board 
 88. The Alternate Board notes that where there are no facts or e v i d e n c e  w h i c h  a r e  c a p able of sufficiently 
substantiating or supporting a reasonable suspicion of partiali ty, a partiality objection cannot be upheld. Merely 
because a party in the appeal proceedings purports to have a su spicion of partiality, or purports to continue to have 
a suspicion of partiality, does not mean that the necessary req uirements, as set out in, amongst others, Article 48 

  
 13 / 13 
of the Basic Regulation, are met. Moreover, and in any event, t he Alternate Board notes that, according to the case-
law of the EPO Boards of Appeal, which may apply by analogy to the CPVO Board of Appeal, partiality objections 
involving procedural deficiencies are generally only capable of  supporting a decision to remove or reject a member 
of the EPO Boards of Appeal, if they “ result from…a preconceived atti tude or deliberate intention ” (See Case T 
1257/14 of 5 February 2018, ECLI:EP:BA:2018:T125714.20180205). As the Alternate Board has already concluded, 
the facts in this case are not capable of supporting or substan tiating a reasonable suspicion of partiality. This is 
particularly in light of the corr ective action taken by the Boa rd of Appeal on 17 May 2022 and thereafter, which the 
Alternate Board considers transparent, decisive and proportiona te. That action in particular, removes beyond doubt, 
any possible suspicion of partiality vis-à-vis the Chairperson,  in particular any possible suspicion that procedural 
deficiencies were the result of a preconceived attitude or deli berate intention.  
 
89. On the basis of the above, the Alternate Board concludes th at the third plea is not well founded and must 
therefore be dismissed.  
VI. Decision as to the Action to be Taken: 
  
90. The CPVO Board of Appeal, in its alternate composition, ord ers that: 
- The Objection be dismissed. - The Chairperson not be removed.  - In light of the Appellant’s withdrawal of its request for an oral hearing before being informed of all the necessary 
facts and circumstances, the Alternate Board recommends that th e parties in the appeal proceedings be invited to 
attend an oral hearing, and an oral hearing be convened, should  the parties and/or the Board of Appeal consider it 
necessary. In the event an oral hearing is convened, the Altern ate Board recommend that the parties be invited to 
submit new pleading notes to assist the interpreters.    
Marcus NAVIN-JONES       Dirk REHEUL
